query: weight loss, tachycardia, tremors, heat intolerance - diagnostic criteria for hyperthyroidism

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Disease and Pregnancy -- History and Physical -- Hyperthyroidism
retrieved_docs.content: Similarly, history and physical examination in pregnant women with thyrotoxicosis are indistinguishable from hyperthyroidism in non-pregnant adults. Some symptoms of hyperthyroidism may include palpitations, excessive sweating, heat intolerance, anxiety, insomnia, weight loss, and tremors. Physical examination findings may include tachycardia, lid lag, stare, diaphoresis, and hyperreflexia. Findings specific to Graves disease include diffuse goiter, ophthalmopathy (exophthalmos), and pretibial myxedema.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperthyroidism -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Thyroid hormone has physiological effects on multiple organ systems. As a result, the symptoms and signs of hyperthyroidism involve manifestations from multiple organ systems. Clinical manifestations are associated with a hyperadrenergic and hypermetabolic state. Common manifestations include unintentional weight loss (about 10% of patients can gain weight due to increased appetite), palpitations, tremors, heat intolerance, dyspnea on exertion, increased anxiety, irritability, fatigue, muscle weakness, increased frequency of bowel movements (some patients can have significant diarrhea), hair loss, loss of libido, and oligomenorrhea or amenorrhea in women. [20] [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Secondary Hypertension -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Heat intolerance, weight loss, palpitations, systolic hypertension, exophthalmos, tremor, and tachycardia will occur in hyperthyroidism.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyrotoxicosis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Sinus tachycardia is the most common cardiac rhythm disturbance in thyrotoxicosis; however, atrial fibrillation can also occur, particularly in older patients or those with valvular disease and coronary artery disease. Older individuals often exhibit fewer typical clinical signs and may instead present with cardiac symptoms such as heart failure and arrhythmias, as well as hypercalcemia, depression, fatigue, and weight loss. This presentation is referred to as "apathetic hyperthyroidism" and may be mistaken for age-related conditions. [8] On physical examination, patients with thyrotoxicosis often appear cachectic, hyperthermic, diaphoretic, and anxious. Common findings include: Goiter Palpable nodules Tachycardia or atrial fibrillation Systolic hypertension Dyspnea Abdominal tenderness Hyperreflexia Proximal muscle weakness Tremor Gynecomastia Short stature in affected children Stare Lid lag [2] [4] [11] [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Toxic Goiter -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Diffuse toxic goiter may present with the usual hypermetabolic (like, for example, heat intolerance, sweating, weight loss, etc) and adrenergic symptoms (in particular the palpitations, tremors, emotional lability, etc) of hyperthyroidism, along with the swelling of the goiter. On the other hand, elderly patients may not present with adrenergic symptoms but rather with apathy and atrial fibrillation, which might also represent symptoms of depression, malignancy, or cardiac abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: Graves Disease -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: In younger patients, common presentations include heat intolerance, sweating, fatigue, weight loss, palpitation, hyper defecation, and tremors. Other features include insomnia, anxiety, nervousness, hyperkinesia, dyspnea, muscle weakness, pruritus, polyuria, oligomenorrhea or amenorrhea in the female, loss of libido, and neck fullness. Eye symptoms include lids swelling, ocular pain, conjunctival redness, double vision. Palpable goiter is more common in the younger population, age younger than 60 years.  Up to 10 % of patients may have weight gain. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Toxic Goiter -- History and Physical -- History
retrieved_docs.content: Patients may present with a history of one or more consequences of a hyperthyroid state. These include weight loss, heat intolerance (with other heat-related symptoms like polydipsia and sweating), tremors, nervousness, anxiety, fatigue, palpitations, shortness of breath, frequent defecation or diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, etc. Patients may also complain of an obvious neck swelling or sensation of a lump in the neck, globus, swallowing difficulty, orthopnea, etc. Patients with Graves disease may present with additional features like: Ophthalmopathy, known as Graves orbitopathy (seen in 25% of patients), includes proptosis, diplopia, periorbital edema, excessive lacrimation, etc. Thyroid dermatopathy (rare, only seen in 4% of patients, and usually concurrent with orbitopathy) presents with slightly thickened pigmented skin, especially over the pretibial area. Abnormalities of the reproductive system, most frequently presenting as irregular menstruation. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypothyroid Myopathy -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management
retrieved_docs.content: Symptom assessment: Patients should be monitored for palpitations, tremors, anxiety, insomnia, heat intolerance, sweating, weight loss, and diarrhea.

retrieved_docs.title: Pituitary Adenoma -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: TSH secreting adenoma: Patients have symptoms of palpitations, arrhythmias, and weight loss. On exam, they may have tremors and a goiter.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Thyroid -- Clinical Significance -- Hyperthyroidism
retrieved_docs.content: Excess thyroid hormone production may arise from autoimmune or neoplastic processes. Graves disease accounts for most cases and involves autoantibodies targeting the thyroid-stimulating hormone receptor. [23] Common symptoms include heat intolerance, excessive sweating, tachycardia, palpitations, anxiety, unintentional weight loss, tremor, menstrual irregularities, and ocular findings such as exophthalmos and diplopia. Treatment depends on comorbidities, disease severity, and patient preference, and may include pharmacological or surgical approaches.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperpituitarism -- History and Physical -- Excessive Production of Thyroid-Stimulating Hormone
retrieved_docs.content: Secondary hyperthyroidism is caused by increased secretion of TSH. Clinical features of thyroid hormone excess include weight loss, heat intolerance, anxiety, menstrual disturbances, and palpitations. Hyperthyroid symptoms with TSHomas are often mild to moderate. An enlarged goiter may also be present on physical exam.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperthyroidism (Nursing) -- Assessment
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperthyroidism may manifest as weight loss despite an increased appetite, palpitation, nervousness, tremors, dyspnea, fatigability, diarrhea or increased GI motility, muscle weakness, heat intolerance, and diaphoresis. The signs and symptoms of thyroid hormone exposure to peripheral tissues reflect a hypermetabolic state. A patient with hyperthyroidism classically presents with signs and symptoms that reflect this state of increased metabolic activity. Common symptoms that a patient may report include unintentional weight loss despite unchanged oral intake, palpitations, diarrhea or increased frequency of bowel movements, heat intolerance, diaphoresis, and/or menstrual irregularities.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyrotoxicosis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular: Palpitations, tachycardia, atrial fibrillation, dyspnea, orthopnea, and edema (in the case of congestive heart failure). [5] Neurological: Proximal muscle weakness, brisk deep tendon reflexes with a rapid relaxation phase, and tremors. [11] Gastrointestinal or metabolic: Weight loss, increased appetite, diarrhea, and hyperdefecation. [41] Psychiatric: Anxiety, insomnia, cognitive impairment, and psychosis. [6] [37] [38] Adrenergic stimulation: Heat intolerance, hyperthermia, and increased sweating. [11] Dermatological: Onycholysis, vitiligo, pretibial myxedema, and thyroid acropachy (clubbing of the fingers and toes with swelling of the hands and feet). [42] [43] Ocular: Exophthalmos, increased lacrimation, blurry vision, diplopia, photophobia, decreased color vision, and periorbital edema. [11] Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Thyroid Eye Disease ," for more information. Reproductive: Amenorrhea, oligomenorrhea, and gynecomastia. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Pituitary Hyperplasia in Primary Hypothyroidism -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: If a patient has hypothyroidism, they may experience symptoms such as weight gain, fatigue, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, and irregular menses in females. [8] Children may present with delayed growth, short stature, obesity, and rarely precocious puberty. [9] Since a TSH-producing adenoma is on the differential, hyperthyroid symptoms are always asked in tandem, such as unintentional weight loss, anxiety, experiencing tremors, sweating, heat intolerance, and palpitations. As prolactin levels are fairly commonly elevated in patients with pituitary hyperplasia as a result of primary hypothyroidism, the clinician should also ask about amenorrhea and galactorrhea in females as well as any issues with fertility. For structural concerns related to the enlarged pituitary gland, the clinician should ask about the presence of headaches or changes in vision that would be concerning for the mass abutting the optic chiasm.

retrieved_docs.title: Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Thyroid disease (hyperthyroid or hypothyroid): Hypothyroid symptoms and signs include weight gain, constipation, cold intolerance, depression, dry skin, and delayed deep tendon reflexes. Hyperthyroid signs and symptoms include weight loss, poor sleep, heat intolerance, heart rhythm disturbance such as atrial fibrillation, and hyperreflexia.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Thyroid Stimulating Hormone -- Pathophysiology -- Hyperthyroidism
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperthyroidism is a condition characterized by excessive secretion of thyroid hormones. A variety of medical conditions lead to hyperthyroidism, including Graves' disease, thyroid neoplasm, thyroid adenomas, excess TSH secretion, or exogenous T3 or T4 administration. Symptoms of hyperthyroidism include increased basal metabolic rate, weight loss, increased appetite, sweating, tremors, heat sensitivity, irritability, diarrhea, and insomnia. In primary hyperthyroidism, as in the case of a thyroid adenoma, TSH levels tend to decrease due to negative feedback inhibition exerted on the anterior pituitary by T3 and T4. In secondary hyperthyroidism, as in the case of a TSH or TRH secreting tumor, both TSH and T3/T4 levels increase. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 325
retrieved_docs.content: Antithyroid preparations are pharmacologic agents that counteract excess thyroid hormone production and are employed in the treatment of hyperthyroidism (فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ). By inhibiting the synthesis of thyroid hormones, these medications help normalize elevated metabolic activity, alleviate symptoms such as tachycardia, weight loss, and tremor, and reduce the risk of complications associated with uncontrolled hyperthyroidism. Effective use of antithyroid preparations is therefore a central component of managing patients with فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ.

retrieved_docs.content: plant seeds and flowers). These ancillary ingredients are likely intended to supply nutritional support, antioxidant capacity, and additional pharmacologic actions that complement the primary adrenergic and corticotropic effects. The repeated pattern of pairing epinephrine with corticotropin, together with other endocrine hormones, neurotransmitters, minerals, vitamins, and herbal components, suggests a therapeutic strategy aimed at modulating sympathetic activity, adrenal cortical output, thyroid function, and overall metabolic homeostasis. The inclusion of both synthetic and natural substances across the products underscores a multimodal approach to patient (or animal) care that leverages synergistic actions of catecholamines, ACTH, and supportive nutrients to address conditions such as allergic reactions, neuro‑behavioral imbalances, weight management, and systemic endocrine deficiencies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2216
retrieved_docs.content: Medium chain triglycerides are contraindicated in patients who have ارتفاع نسبة الدهون الثلاثية في الدم (elevated blood triglyceride levels), indicating that the use of medium chain triglycerides should be avoided in individuals with this condition because it may exacerbate or is not recommended for managing high triglyceride concentrations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2201
retrieved_docs.content: Karbimazol, also known as carbimazole, is classified as an antithyroid preparation and is used in the treatment of hyperthyroidism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2074
retrieved_docs.content: Antithyroid preparations are classified as a type of diiodotyrosine, a compound that participates in thyroid hormone synthesis, and they are employed to inhibit thyroid hormone production in the management of hyperthyroid conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2759
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotherapy is regarded as an allowable therapeutic option in several specific clinical contexts. It is permitted for patients presenting with وضعية المَجيء المقعدي, for individuals who have thyroid disorders, and in situations of تهديد بالإجهاض (threatened abortion). This indicates that radiotherapy is not contraindicated in these particular conditions and may be considered appropriate when clinical judgment deems it necessary.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 891
retrieved_docs.content: Thyroid Extracts and Thyroid Inhibitors comprise a category that includes the subclass thyreostatika, which are agents specifically related to the function of the štítná žláza (thyroid gland). Both thyreostatika and the thyroid hormone trijodthyronin (triiodothyronine) are directly associated with the štítná žláza, reflecting their roles in modulating thyroid activity. The štítná žláza is also identified as the anatomical finding site for قصور الدَّرَقِيَّة (hypothyroidism), underscoring the clinical relevance of these substances and the gland in the diagnosis and management of thyroid disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1443
retrieved_docs.content: Antithyroid preparations are a class of medications that inhibit thyroid hormone synthesis, and they encompass specific agents such as diiodotyrosine and carbimazole. Both diiodotyrosine and carbimazole function as antithyroid drugs, sharing the common therapeutic purpose of reducing excessive thyroid hormone production in conditions like hyperthyroidism. Recognizing that these compounds belong to the broader category of antithyroid preparations helps clinicians select appropriate treatment options and understand their pharmacologic relationships.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1828
retrieved_docs.content: The conditions نوبة (seizure), خلل الحركة المتأخر (late‑onset movement disorder), فطار غزوي, intestinal infection, and إجهاض تلقائي غير كامل (incomplete spontaneous abortion) are all recognized as entities that can be qualified as chemically induced. Identifying a chemical trigger as a possible cause is important for the evaluation and management of these diverse clinical presentations, because exposure to certain substances may precipitate seizures, movement abnormalities, gastrointestinal infections, and incomplete abortions. Awareness of the chemically induced nature of these conditions helps clinicians consider appropriate diagnostic testing, preventive strategies, and targeted therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2517
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of thyroid is classified as a type of اضطراب صماوي, indicating that it belongs to the broader category of endocrine disorders. By recognizing thyroid cancer as an endocrine disorder, clinicians understand that the disease originates in the hormone‑producing thyroid gland and shares pathophysiological features with other conditions affecting the endocrine system. This classification guides diagnostic evaluation, treatment planning, and prognostic assessment within the context of endocrine pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 435
retrieved_docs.content: Levothyroxine sodium, potassium perchlorate, and dextrothyroxine sodium are each classified as thyroid preparations, which themselves are a subset of the broader category of thyroxin. As specific thyroid preparation agents, these compounds are used to influence thyroid hormone activity and are relevant in the therapeutic management of thyroid disorders. Recognizing that all three agents belong to the thyroid preparation group, and that this group falls under the larger class of thyroxin, helps clinicians understand their pharmacologic relationships and select appropriate treatments for patients with hypothyroidism, hyperthyroidism, or other thyroid-related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2174
retrieved_docs.content: Thyroid extracts and thyroid inhibitors represent a therapeutic class of agents that affect thyroid function, and within this class the specific subgroup known as thyreostatika is included. Thyreostatika are medications that act to modulate or suppress thyroid activity, and they are classified as a child category of the broader group of thyroid extracts and thyroid inhibitors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 5 -- Community 2957
retrieved_docs.content: Diet therapy is recognized as an allowed qualifier for managing نوبة (seizure) and عضال غدي (glandular disease), indicating that dietary interventions are considered appropriate therapeutic options for these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2802
retrieved_docs.content: Alzheimer‑type dementia, glandular disease, and hyperthyroidism are all conditions that can be influenced by genetic factors. Genetic predisposition is recognized as an important contributor to the development of Alzheimer‑type dementia, affecting the risk and progression of this neurodegenerative disorder. Similarly, certain glandular diseases have hereditary components that may affect gland function and pathology. Excess thyroid activity, or hyperthyroidism, also exhibits a genetic basis, with inherited variations influencing susceptibility to thyroid hormone overproduction. Understanding the genetic underpinnings of these disorders aids in risk assessment, early detection, and the development of personalized therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1560
retrieved_docs.content: Antithyroid preparations, exemplified by the drug karbimazol (carbimazole), function by interfering with the synthesis of diiodotyrosine, a crucial intermediate in thyroid hormone production. In clinical use, these antithyroid agents are associated with the condition described as فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ (excess of blood or hyperemia), which may appear as a physiological response or side effect. Recognizing that karbimazol belongs to the broader class of antithyroid preparations and that its mechanism involves diiodotyrosine helps clinicians effectively treat hyperthyroid disorders while monitoring for signs of hyperemia.
